paragraphs.body: In the article, “Cheerleading: A Prevalence of catastrophic injuries” Brittany Bowyer argues that cheerleading is one of the most dangerous sports. She explains her point by developing several important ideas. First, Bowyer addresses the high risk of severe injuries that can come with being a part of this sport. She points out that “The sport was responsible for 65 percent of direct catastrophic injuries to female high school athletes during a 27-year period.” according to the American Academy of Pediatrics. Next, she emphasizes these injuries' personal and long-lasting impacts on the athletes. She shows us this by interviewing many current, and former cheerleaders and their parents on the injuries that have happened to them and their children. Finally, Bowyer touches on the sport’s lack of recognition and safety. Many people don’t recognize cheerleading as being a contact sport, but Bowyer shows us that it is just that. Bowyer herself interviews a former cheerleader, “My X-rays showed that I had broken three vertebrae in my neck, and I was one vertebra away from being paralyzed from the waist down.” Basically, Bowyer is showing us that cheerleading can easily become a contact sport. In my opinion, bowyer’s main point is to raise awareness for the lack of protection and awareness that cheerleading gets.

paragraphs.body: In this article, Bowyer gives specific examples of incidents involving competitive cheerleaders. She believes that cheer should be considered a real sport and wants to bring more attention to safety when performing. Bowyer states, “Cheerleading accounted for 65.2 percent of all catastrophic injuries in youth sports.” Putting this research in her article Bowyer shows that cheerleading is more dangerous than most sports. She argues that cheerleading should be taken seriously and challenges the reputation of cheerleading not being a sport.

paragraphs.body: From a young age, I was always doing cartwheels down a hill and somersaulting in my room. After watching me do this for years my mom had the bright idea to enroll me into a gymnastics program. I immediately loved it. Eventually, I outgrew it, so I decided to quit. At that time, I was in the eighth grade, getting ready for high school. I needed something to do and that’s when my mom suggested cheerleading. It was the perfect alternative. I already knew how to do all the tumbling from gymnastics and how hard could it be? I couldn’t be more wrong.

paragraphs.body: I want to make it clear that I’m talking about competitive cheer, not game day. What’s the difference? Game day is jumping around and cheering for a sport, maybe a stunt here and there, which is what most people think when talking about cheer but generally doesn’t require much athleticism. Competitive cheer is less common. Physically competing skills and routines against other teams. I always feel the need to elaborate on the difference because of the reputation that cheerleading gets as an unimportant and flippant extracurricular activity. What is in a competitive cheer routine? Of course, there’s tumbling, jumps, and dancing, but the most important and what I think is the most challenging is stunting. Stunting is where a group of people lift and toss people in the air while simultaneously synchronizing movements. Bases need to have strong upper body strength while the flyers need flexibility, balance, and core strength. Not only do cheerleaders have to be physically strong but mentally also. It takes complete trust within the whole stunt group to pull off any skill. Each person must do their own job but also be aware of what everyone else is doing all at the same time. Being focused and aware is crucial for stunting, any distraction or mistake can result in serious injury.

paragraphs.body: For my economics class last year all the students had to split up into groups and interview people with random questions. A popular question was is cheer a sport? Most of the people just smirked and quickly said no. I understand their point of view. One of the reasons cheerleaders have these stereotypes of being ditsy or dumb is because of how they are projected in movies. Many of the characters playing the roles of cheerleaders play them as ditzy airheads or sexualized objects. For example, in the movie “Bring It On”, the captains of the cheerleading team are very rude, short-tempered, and dumb. Hollywood is no help with helping change the stigma around cheerleaders. In the column cheerleading isn’t a sport, it’s an Olympic sport, Zach Edmondson himself writes “If the cheerleaders are simply shouting and throwing their arms around every time, I look at them during football games, then I wouldn’t think what they do is athletic. Some school’s cheer squads perform routines that would make you think there was another team competing with them though, and that is hard for me to discredit as something less than a sport.” Basically, Edmondson is saying that cheerleading squads perform routines that are so impressive it’s hard to call that not a and what is your point about his ideas? What do you have to say sport.

paragraphs.body: In the article “Cheerleading: A Prevalence of Catastrophic Injuries” Bowler highlights the dangers of cheerleading. This goes along with cheerleading being underappreciated and not taken seriously and because of that safety precautions are slim to none. In my own beliefs, the reason for this is because of the reputation cheer gets. I was part of a back-to-back state and a 2024 WPIAL championship-winning team, so I feel qualified enough to speak on the challenges in this sport. I’d have practice every Monday, Tuesday, Wednesday, and Thursday and have a game on Friday and then an endurance training on Sunday. Having to practice that much came with injuries happening through the whole team frequently. The most common being sprained ankles. By the end of the season, every flyer on the team had an ankle brace on. There was one girl who fractured her back, another who had a sprained wrist, a third had to get hip surgery. In Bowyer’s article, she shares the story of a former cheerleader. “Alexa was kicked in the left temple. She didn’t know that this would be the first of three concussions that would eventually lead to her retirement from cheer.” By adding this story Bowyer is showing us how easy it is to get seriously injured. I also had something similar happen to me. In the tenth grade, my base slipped from under me and my knee landed right in her face. Later that night she had to get two stitches in her lip. That was the first time I realized the importance of safety and knowing what to do when falling from a stunt.

annotations.text: Having to practice that much came with injuries happening through the whole team frequently.
one girl who fractured her back,

paragraphs.body: How can we improve the safety in cheerleading? A few Ideas come to mind. The first is Proper training. Having proper training is crucial to staying safe. Coaches with the proper training can teach cheerleaders how to catch and spot. They also can point out when there is a weak point in the stunt and fix it before it becomes an issue. A second safety precaution is a helmet. These helmets have become more and more popular in the cheer world. These are foam helmets that the whole stunt group would wear so that the flyer has protection from falling and hitting her head on the ground and for the base’s protection from getting hit and fallen on. Lastly, I find it very important for coaches to go through training before they are allowed to coach. Proper training helps to ensure the safety and wellbeing of the cheerleaders.
